g.data-narration: A thread library provides the programmer with an API for creating and managing threads. Two implementation approaches: user-space library or kernel-level library.
g.text: API for creating and managing threads in applications

g.data-narration: POSIX Pthreads: the standard UNIX threading API. Specification, not implementation. Available on Linux, macOS, Solaris. Uses pthread_create, pthread_join, pthread_exit.
Windows Threads API: uses CreateThread function. Similar concepts to Pthreads but different function names. WaitForSingleObject replaces join. CloseHandle for cleanup.
Java Threads: managed by JVM. Create by extending Thread class or implementing Runnable. Thread.start begins execution. Platform-independent threading.
User-space library: entire library in user space. No kernel involvement for thread management. Fast context switch. But blocking one thread blocks all since kernel sees one thread.
g.text: All code and data in user space
No kernel calls for thread mgmt

g.data-narration: Kernel-level library: supported directly by OS. Thread creation and scheduling in kernel space. Slower management but true concurrency. Used by modern Pthreads and Windows.
Threading issues: fork and exec in threaded programs. Thread cancellation can be async or deferred. Signal handling: which thread receives signals. Thread-local storage.
g.text: fork() in threads | Cancellation (async/deferred) | Signal handling | Thread-local storage | Scheduler activations

g.data-narration: Three major thread libraries: Pthreads for UNIX, Win32 for Windows, Java for JVM. Choice of user vs kernel implementation affects parallelism and performance.
g.text: Pthreads (UNIX) | Win32 Threads (Windows) | Java Threads (JVM) - all provide create, join, exit
